Level 460: The Lost Fragment

[[File:Sty460Hole.jpeg|600px|center|link=]]
[LEVEL 460] — Quantum Hole
A rare quantum hole that appears in the level that helps you leave the level




It was quite distinct.

This place was the remorse of the shadows

None had ever stepped into

None had ever fallen into the abyss

None but nothing.

Something was here,

Something real… something… something… something…

The fear. The memories. My consciousness,

This place… they… feast on them.




[[File:ArrowMove.gif|center|300px]]
Date: Oct 15th 2000 | 00:39:59 Prode’s Log #1
CLICK ME
The big emptiness of Level 0 stretched out all around. A boy lays there, not moving. Prode woke up flat on a corridor floor that felt unfamiliar, he was not scared but he was confused. In Level 0, nothing ever really stood out. Overhead, long fluorescent tubes gave off that bored, lonely buzz, humming the same grating note into air that never felt quite fresh. He kept his cheek pressed to the ground for a second, just watching the light sputter at the far end of the corridor. Then he sat up, quick hands checking he still had his few belongings, and then he got to his feet. So, he walked. That’s all you really could do here. Just walk and hope and don’t think too much about whether you were actually headed somewhere or just circling around inside an endless loop. By now, he knew better than to rush or get too determined. The harder you tried to find a way out, the more the place twisted itself up. It just kept spitting out the same halls, the same walls, the same stale, heavy air. So Prode walked without any real plan. He let his hand trail along the wallpaper, not even thinking about it. The lights kept buzzing above him. The old carpet muffled every step, soft and thick, like Level 0 was trying to quietly soak up and erase his presence as he moved. He turned one corner, then another. Something started to feel off. Not in a way he could name; more like the subtle shift you notice when someone moves a tiny object in a room. Somehow, the wallpaper seemed farther away. The ceiling had climbed higher. The hum overhead deepened, just a fraction, slipping into a lower, almost human kind of drone. Prode slowed down. Glanced at the floor. The carpet looked the same, but it felt different—thinner, sort of faded, more like a memory of carpet than the real thing. He kept moving. Nothing dramatic happened. No bright flash. No noise, no sense of a door opening, or a line being crossed. Just a step. One moment the world was close and yellow, the ceiling buzzing. The next step, no ceiling. No walls. The carpet had vanished. Silence pressed in, but the hum remained—now huge, everywhere at once and nowhere at all. Level 0 was gone. He found himself standing somewhere completely new, and just like that, Level 460 began.
File:460 User:{{USERNAME}} Sty

Description

[[File:Sty460Something.jpeg|link=|600px]]
[LEVEL 460] — Outside Drawing
A drawing found in Level 460 that could be possibly drew out by a kid
Level 460 is a vast, non-Euclidean expanse that defies straightforward cartographic documentation. Unlike many levels of the Backrooms, it does not present the familiar architecture of office corridors or yellowed fluorescent hallways. Instead, it manifests as something between a landscape and a hallucination, an endless, mist-laden terrain of fragmented structures that loom upward without any adherence to physical law. The environment presents itself in two dimensions rendered through what seems like an obsolete graphical engine. Buildings, towers, and skeletal formations rise from a flat, featureless ground plane in stark, wireframe silhouettes — the aesthetic of a forgotten video game engine from an era that may or may not have existed. Perspective here is broken in the way that only dreams and low-resolution renders can manage: structures appear simultaneously distant and close, large and miniature, threatening yet meaningless. Wanderers consistently report the sensation of looking at a world that is 2D rendered and similar to those of post-retro video game graphics. The atmosphere carries a perpetual soft static, like a monitor left on in an empty room late at night. The ambient noise is sometimes described as a hum, sometimes as a breath held for too long. Lighting has no apparent source. Shadows exist where they have no logical right to. Despite this, visibility is never truly obscured as the level seems to resist the mercy of darkness, ensuring that whatever is present can always be seen, and that whatever is watching can always see back. There is no weather. There is no wind. There is no hunger, at first. What there is, however, is the persistent sensation that the level is remembering you — cataloguing the texture of your thoughts with each step you take further in.

"''It wasn't the buildings that bothered me. Buildings are buildings, even fake ones. It was the way the space between them felt full. Full of something that had no shape yet.''" '''— AVEN Field Report #C-19, Testimony of Wanderer'''

[[File:ArrowMove.gif|center|300px]]
Date: Oct 15th 2000 | 1:02:22 Prode’s Monologue #1
CLICK ME
For a long time, I just stood there. Completely still. You know that feeling when something startles you so much that your body can’t quite process it yet? That was me. I couldn’t even name what I was looking at. The ground was the first thing that caught my eye. Utterly flat, unnaturally so. It stretched endlessly in every direction. No rise, no dip, just this endless plain that didn’t feel like it was made out of anything, like I did not even know if it exists. And all around me, things rose up: structures, buildings, or the suggestions of buildings. They looked like what you might end up sketching if someone described “a building” to you but you’d never actually seen one. Just lines. Thin, bright lines of light, all sharp corners and angles, floating against a background that wasn’t black or white or any color at all…it was like the world’s screensaver had come on and nobody’d noticed yet. At first glance, these things looked almost real. Like you could walk right in and find a room, or lean against a wall. But the harder I tried to focus, the more wrong they felt. My eyes kept sliding off the shapes. A tower seemed tall one second, then not tall at all, and I swear they shifted without moving. I tried to make sense of it, but my brain just refused. This place ignored how things are supposed to work. If someone made it, they weren’t building for people. While all this was happening, a low hum buzzed into my ear—steady, always there, not really coming from anywhere. It felt woven into the place, the way emptiness rings in your ears. I just stood there. I waited for something to break the silence, and the silence waited me out.
File:460 User:{{USERNAME}} Sty

Entities

[[File:Sty460out.jpeg|600px|link=]]
[LEVEL 460] — Outside Image
An image of Level 460’s enviroment
The only confirmed entities within Level 460 are known as the Hollows. They are ubiquitous to this level and have not been documented anywhere else within the Backrooms with any reliability, though AVEN researchers have proposed theoretical links between the Hollows and certain void-class entities encountered in adjacent levels. The Hollows are non-three-dimensional entities, they exist within three-dimensional space but present themselves as two-dimensional constructions. They appear as humanoid outlines of coloured light: tall, thin, composed entirely of glowing edges with no filled interior. They are transparent. You can see straight through them to whatever lies behind, and what lies behind is never obscured, only contextualised strangely by the fact of their presence. Wanderers do report that they can feel the presence of Hollows even though they do not see them. Their color varies: some glow in red, yellow, blue and most glow in white. Whether this variation carries individual significance or serves merely as a way differentiate among them is unknown. What is noted in almost every documented encounter is that each Hollow, regardless of colour, faintly illuminates the immediate area around itself — not helpfully, but in the manner of a dying screen in a dark room, casting more unease than light. The Hollows stand at an average height of 5'9", though this varies for each Hollow, their proportions are deeply wrong in ways that are difficult to articulate in formal documentation. Their limbs are elongated and misaligned. Their posture suggests the memory of a human stance rather than the genuine positioning. They are recognisably like a person you have known before, yet disportioncate and uncanny. They are encountered most frequently in pairs or small clusters of two to three. A solitary Hollow is an unusual sight, and Wanderers who have reported lone Hollow sightings have, without exception, described the experience as being more unsettling than any group encounter. There is something about a thing like that being alone that registers as wrong in a way that defies explanation. The Hollows do not move. In every documented encounter, they hold their position without deviation. They are present, watching and utterly still. They have never been observed to relocate between one moment and the next. They are simply there, and then, if you are lucky, at some later point they are simply not there, and you cannot account for when the transition occurred.
note1.txt
note2.txt
note3.txt
note4.txt
Hover your mouse on one of the buttons to display information.
A hollow would inconsistently shift it’s appearance without warning on some occasions. For a span of seconds, its outline will reshape itself into something else entirely: a silhouette that is recognisable. A family member. A friend not thought of in years. Someone whose face carries, even in pure outline, the specific weight of love or grief or unresolved guilt. Equally, the shift may produce something else, a shape that belongs to a nightmare, something the mind had constructed in the dark hours and hoped never to see standing upright in daylight. The episodes are brief, lasting only a few seconds before the Hollow returns to its neutral, distorted form. Witnesses describe these moments as feeling longer than they actually are, with the instant of recognition seeming to stretch and distort. Interestingly, the return to normality does not bring any sense of relief.
*Initial exposure: mild disorientation, difficulty maintaining a continuous train of thought, a sense of being watched from inside one's own skull.


*Prolonged exposure: significant short-term memory loss; episodes of shell shock; heightened emotional distress relating to repressed or forgotten memories.


*Extended exposure: Full vegetative state. The affected Wanderer is entirely unresponsive, non-ambulatory, and not consciously aware of the surroundings.


Wanderers who repeatedly attempt to interact with the Hollows, or who stay in close proximity to them for a prolonged duration, are subjected to a cognitive hazard, which is not transmitted through sound, touch, or eye contact alone, with AVEN researchers theorizing it as environment hazard generated by the entity’s presence, akin to radiation in this context. If one remains in close proximity with the Hollows, they may experience memory loss, and if given enough time, they may even enter a vegetative state.

According to AVEN field data, a Wanderer who has entered a vegetative state has a window of reversibility for about four to six hours. But this number isn't always right. During this time, the state can be broken and reversed by being taken away from the Hollow and having a friend keep it going. The vegetative state will last forever after this time is up. People who are affected will die from dehydration and malnutrition over the course of weeks if they don't get help from someone else.
After death, whatever happens in Level 460 is documented and very disturbing. The body remains, biologically it undergoes no decay that is discernible to the naked eye for a period of days, but it becomes less substantial, less defined and less present, the outline remaining after the insides have disappeared like it has dissolved into the floor of the level, the colour drained and replaced by a faint pulsating luminescence and finally in the last stage, the body rises and remains suspended at the height it has attained, immobile, motionless and inert.
[[File:ArrowMove.gif|center|300px]]


Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation (AVEN)

[[File:Sty460buns.jpeg|600px|link=]]
[LEVEL 460] — Picture of Food
An image of the only food source in Level 460
The only human presence in Level 460 is a station run by the Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation also known as the AVEN. The Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation does not say where the station is in any recorded findings. This is probably because Level 460 is very strange and can change distances and spaces without warning. What we do know is that the station is in an area of Level 460 where things are stable. In this area the unusual things that happen in Level 460 do not seem to apply. The buildings are normal. You do not hear the weird humming noise as much. The Hollows also do not manifest anywhere near this safe location. The researchers from the Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation have not been able to find an answer on why this is this since they set up the station. The Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation station in Level 460 is a base and a safe haven for people who are lost and need help. People who find the station, which usually happens by accident are given food, a place to rest medical help and a thorough interview. The Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation is not just helping people out of kindness because they also ask everyone who passes through the station about their experiences. This helps the Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation learn more about the Hollows and how they behave. The main goal of the Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation is to deal with entities in the Backrooms. Level 460 is one of the places where the Agency for Void Entity Neutralisation works and the Hollows are one of the well-documented entities. The Agency, for Void Entity Neutralisation has not said much about an entity that cannot be touched or understood and which might be made up of the memories of people who died in the Backrooms.
===LOG ENTRY P-#12===
''(Recorder engages. Extended static — approximately forty seconds. Static resolves as device calibrates.)'' ''(Chair scrapes against a hard floor. A pause.)'' '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Hello, Mr Burgers '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' ... '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Are you feeling better now? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Yes. I am. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Good. We'll begin when you're ready. ''(A long pause. The hum of the station fills the silence.)'' '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:'''I- I am ready. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Did you experience anything you would describe as unusual? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Is that even a question, Doctor? You know the answer to that. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' I do. I apologise. It's a formality. ''(pause)'' What did you see in there? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Lines. A lot of lines. It was like a fever dream… weird houses and towers, but flat. Like someone had described architecture to a machine and the machine had done its best. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And the entities? Did you encounter any of the Hollows? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Those outline things. Yes. ''(pause)'' They looked familiar. I recognised them. I don't mean the shape of them — I mean I ''recognised'' them. Like you'd recognise someone you haven't seen in years by the way they stand. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' What did you do when you say you recognised them? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I tried to get closer. ''(long pause)'' And then I looked to my left and watched a man drop. He just went down. He was near a group of three of them and he just dropped and started convulsing and didn't stop. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' That must have been deeply distressing. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I'm… fine. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' ''(quietly)'' You've said that twice now. ''(No response from the Wanderer. The station hum echoes throughout the room.)'' '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' How did you find the safe zone? It's a rare occurrence, the geometry of this level doesn't lend itself to deliberate navigation. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I wasn't navigating. I was just walking. And then it was there. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' We've had similar accounts. Most people who find us describe exactly that. ''(makes a note)'' Last question. How did you leave the level? How did you escape? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' ''(pause)'' I saw a hole. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' A hole? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Like one of those wormhole diagrams. But one-sided. Only going one way. No depth to it, just — an opening. A tear in the render. I touched it. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And then you were here. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' And then I was here. ''(Recorder cuts abruptly. No further audio recovered.)''
===LOG ENTRY P-#57===
''(Recorder engages. A brief crackle of static, then silence.)'' ''(The sound of a cup being set on a table. Liquid being poured. A long pause.)'' '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Take your time. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I'm fine. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' You don't seem to be. ''(pause)'' Whenever you're ready. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Alright. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' When you got in there, what did you do first? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Looked for shelter. You don't stand out in the open anywhere in this hellhole, that’s what I learnt. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And the buildings — were they accessible? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I wasn't sure they'd be solid. They looked like drawings. But I put my hand against one and the wall was there. That surprised me more than anything else in that place, honestly. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' What was it like inside? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Like being inside a drawing and a video game. The walls still had those lines running through them, like the building was showing you its own bones. You could see the interior’s. No furniture. Nothing on the walls. Ceilings that were low in one room and then enormous in the next for no reason. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Did the layout stay consistent? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' No. I'd enter a room through a doorway, turn around to leave, and the doorway would just be wall. So I stopped retracing my steps. I only moved forward. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' You arrived here dehydrated but not starving. How did you manage food? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' The buildings had these — things in them. Black balls, sitting on the floor. Not everywhere. You had to look. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Can you describe them? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Round. Dark. About the size of a fist. Smooth skin on them, slightly shiny. Like those Chinese buns, the steamed ones. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Like the Chinese Steamed Buns? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Yes. Exactly like that. Same surface, same weight. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Did you eat them? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Eventually. I was hungry enough. ''(pause)'' They tasted like nothing. Not bad, not strange. Just — nothing. Like food that only knew how to do the bare minimum. But the hunger went away after. So I kept eating them whenever I found them. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Our intake record mentions companions. You arrived alone. ''(Silence.)'' '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' There were three of us going in. Myself, a woman named Sera, and a man whose name I never actually learned. We hadn't been together before the noclip happened, we just managed to find eachother. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' What happened to them? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Sera was the first to you know…. We found a lone Hollow in one of the corridors. We were giving it plenty of space, moving around it carefully. But she slowed down when she passed it. Started staring at it. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Did she say anything? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' She said ''I know who that is.'' I told her it wasn't anyone and pulled her away. ''(pause)'' But she wasn't right after that. She forgot small things at first. Then bigger things. Her words started going. She'd reach for a sentence and lose it halfway through. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' The Hazard… '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I know that now. ''(pause)'' On the third day she sat down in the middle of a room and didn't get up. Eyes open. Nothing behind them. We stayed with her as long as we could. Then we had to keep moving. ''(Silence.)'' '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And your other companion? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' He lasted longer. He never looked at them directly. He said direct eye contact was what did it. I don't know if he was right. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' What happened to him? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' We came out between two buildings and there were seven Hollows in the open space. We tried to go around them. Wide arc, plenty of distance. ''(pause)'' He stumbled near the end of it. Just a stumble — nothing on the ground, nothing to catch on. But he turned and faced them directly when he went down. All seven of them at once. He dropped before I could reach him. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Immediately? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' Immediately. No sound, no warning. Just down. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' Did he recover? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' For a while I thought so. He got up and was ready to flee alongside me. But then he started going through the same thing Sera went through. Until he was just sitting there with no consciousness. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And then? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I woke up one morning and he was still sitting up straight. Not breathing right. I called his name. Touched his shoulder while avoiding contact with the group of Hollows. Nothing. ''(pause)'' And then he just became less. The lines came first, along the edges of him. Then the inside of him started to clear. Everything that had been him just lifted away until there was only the outline left. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' I'm sorry. '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' I left the building after that. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' And you kept going? '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' There wasn't anything else to do. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' One last question. Did you ever see a Hollow shift into someone you recognised? ''(There was a long silence for forty-three seconds.)'' '''[Unnamed Wanderer]:''' No, I have not. '''[Dr. Stygian]:''' I see. ''(Recorder clicks off.)'' ''(No further audio recovered.)''
[[File:ArrowMove.gif|center|300px]]
Date: Oct 15th 2000 | 3:59:20 Prode’s Monologue #2
CLICK ME
This creeping feeling snuck up on me. It was worse because it took its time. At first, it was just that prickle along the back of your neck, the kind you get when you’re sure someone’s behind you in an empty room. I turned around—twice. There was nothing but flat ground, those wireframe towers, and that empty, switched-off sky. The feeling didn’t go, though. It pressed in, growing heavier, until it felt like the air itself was searching through me. That’s when the memories started to leak in. Not some sudden flood. More like turning a page in a book and finding an old photograph inside. My mother, folding laundry on a Sunday morning—the sound, the smell of clean sheets. My dad in the back yard at dusk, mug of tea cooling in his hand, looking out at nothing in particular. Ordinary things. Memories so private and random I’d almost forgotten were mine. This place found them. Somehow, I was sure of it but it was searching for the softest, most unguarded parts of me, holding them up to the light. And I just stood there, wide open. I wasn’t scared, at least not yet. Just absolutely exposed. It wasn’t cruel, but the intent I feel was strange and unnerving. Then I saw them, and everything else inside me went quiet. Three figures, standing between those wire towers about thirty feet away. Tall, taller than people, but still shaped like people in the weird way a stretched shadow looks kind of familiar. They were nothing but outlines, glowing edges with nothing inside. I could see right through them, which should have made them look less real, but it made them feel even more solid somehow. I had somehow mistaken them for survivors of this place at first but when I realised, I was filled to the brim with fear. My mind was rapidly searching for answers. “What the hell is that”, “Why do they look like that”, “Who are they”, all these questions roused up within me. Looking at them, I felt something I still can’t name. It was somewhere between recognizing an old friend and realizing you’d never see them again. Like standing outside your old house, knowing you can’t go home. Sure, I was afraid but I gradually calmed down as it seemed like they could not move. One of them changed. Just for a few seconds, the lines bent and shifted and suddenly it was my father standing there. Exactly how he stood—leaning slightly forward, like he was just about to speak. I swear my throat closed. I didn’t move. I stopped breathing. Then it was gone—just the creature again, weird and angular and empty, as if nothing had happened, as if it hadn’t just reached inside me and pulled something precious out to show me. I stood there for a long time, feeling something heavy in my chest that I couldn’t put a name to. I was attracted to it, and I wanted to take a closer look. My curiosity got the better of me and I started to walk towards them.
File:460 User:{{USERNAME}} Sty

Stygian’s Verdict

[[File:Sty460Inside.jpeg|link=|600px]]
[LEVEL 460] — Inside Picture
A picture from inside one of the buildings.
“I have been here long enough now to stop being surprised by this level and start being troubled by it in quieter, more specific ways. The first thing I have revised my position on is the Hollows. For a long time the standing consensus within AVEN was that their stillness was simply a property of their nature, that they were passive, ambient, environmental hazards in the way that a gas leak is a hazard. Not dangerous, just a property that they exhibit. I no longer believe that. They don't move because they don't need to. Every information I have collected, and I have collected over four hundred and sixty of them now, describes the same progression. A Wanderer sees a Hollow. The Hollow is still. The Wanderer feels recognition, or grief, or curiosity. The Wanderer moves closer. The Hollow never once closes the distance itself. It simply waits, and the shape it wears is always exactly the right shape to make you want to come to it. The stillness is not passivity. It was……… patience. It is the oldest kind of trap the kind so simple, it makes you walk into it yourself and believe the whole time that it was your own idea. The second thing that occupies me is the level itself or what it once was, or what it is trying to be. The more time I spend here the more convinced I become that Level 460 is not a purely just a void. It is a remnant. The buildings are wrong and the proportions are broken and nothing is furnished or finished, but there is an arrangement to it. Houses that were once houses. Spaces between structures that were once intended as gathering places. The ghost of a layout that once had civic intention behind it. Whatever existed here had density to it. A population, a function, a reason for its own existence. Something happened to that. Something stripped it down to the outline of itself and left the skeleton standing. What remains is not nothing. It is the memory of something, rendered in the only material that survived whatever erasure took place. And that material, increasingly, I believe I can identify. My colleagues in the AVEN research division have been circulating preliminary findings on a theoretical substance that I named Particle:Nothing. The complex version, as I understand it: Particle:Nothing is not an absence of matter but a form of matter that exists in a state of negation. It has mass and occupies space but actively resists conventional physical properties, it does not reflect light normally, does not interact with other matter in predictable ways, and appears to absorb and retain certain forms of energy indefinitely, including, most relevantly, cognitive and emotional residue. Consciousness, in other words. Memory. Feeling. The things that Level 460 demonstrably collects from every Wanderer who passes through it. To put it in short, Just as light implies the existence of darkness, tangibility and matter implies the existence of something else. That something else is called PARTICLE:NOTHING. If the structures of Level 460 are composed in whole or in part of Particle:Nothing, it would account for nearly everything we observe here. The way the level reads its inhabitants. The way the Hollows are formed from what is left behind when a person's consciousness is fully absorbed. The way the buildings feel aware. The way the whole level hums with something that is not electricity and not wind but seems, somehow, to be attention. This is theoretical. I want to be clear about that. But I have been here for fourteen months and I have stopped being comfortable dismissing it. What I keep returning to, at the end of all of it, is this: Something lived here once. Something built here, gathered here, existed here in sufficient density to leave a shape behind. The level is what remains after everything that gave it life was taken away or chose to leave, or was consumed by the very substance it was built from. The Hollows are not foreign to this place. They are its original inhabitants, or what those inhabitants became after long enough exposure to a level made of matter that feeds on consciousness.”

Stands meaning in this stagnant core,
For that they prelude within our very lore,
They shall know why emptiness tore.
